card.intuition: Three different levels of certainty provided by a message broker regarding whether and how many times a message will reach its destination.
card.analogy: Ordering a pizza: 'At-most-once' is they might forget your order. 'At-least-once' is they might send the same pizza twice because they didn't hear you say 'Got it!'. 'Exactly-once' is the perfect delivery.
when_to_use: At-most-once: For non-critical data like high-frequency sensor metrics (losing one doesn't matter)
At-least-once: Most common for business tasks where processing the same thing twice is okay (if idempotent)
Exactly-once: For financial transactions where a double-delivery is a disaster (expensive/slow to implement)
gotchas: Exactly-once delivery is a 'hard problem' in distributed systems and usually carries a high performance penalty
Most systems aim for 'At-least-once' and solve duplicate issues using Idempotent consumers

steps.annotation: System overview — step through to trace the flow.
Producer sends message, waits for broker ack (at-least-once if acks=all).
Broker persists to replicated log before acking.
Consumer processes and commits offset. Uncommitted = redelivered on restart.
After max retries, unprocessable messages routed to dead-letter queue.

exercise.test_code:
assert "most" in guarantees.values() or "MOST" in guarantees.values()
assert "least" in guarantees.values() or "LEAST" in guarantees.values()
